heroOverview: Hypersplenism is a state of exaggerated splenic activity in which an enlarged spleen traps and destroys blood cells — red cells, white cells, or platelets — faster than the bone marrow can replace them. The result is one or more cytopenias, most often thrombocytopenia or anaemia, despite a bone marrow that is normal or hyperactive on biopsy. Hypersplenism is a pathophysiological diagnosis, not a disease in itself; it occurs as a secondary consequence of the many conditions that cause splenomegaly, liver disease causing portal hypertension being the most frequent worldwide. Estimates suggest hypersplenism affects up to 50-70% of patients with cirrhosis and clinically significant portal hypertension. Management is directed at the underlying cause; splenectomy or splenic embolisation is reserved for symptomatic cytopenias that do not respond to other measures.

keyStats.prevalence: Occurs in 50-70% of patients with cirrhosis and portal hypertension; exact global prevalence unknown but tens of millions affected via liver disease alone
keyStats.demographics: All ages; most common in adults with liver disease; also seen in children with haematological malignancy or storage diseases
keyStats.avgAge: Depends on underlying cause; peak in middle-aged adults with chronic liver disease
keyStats.globalCases: Liver cirrhosis alone affects over 100 million people worldwide, making hypersplenism one of the most prevalent haematological complications of chronic liver disease

definition: Hypersplenism (ICD-10: D73.1) describes the syndrome of splenomegaly accompanied by one or more peripheral blood cytopenias — anaemia, leukopenia, or thrombocytopenia — that are correctable by splenectomy and occur in the setting of normal or hyperplastic bone marrow. The mechanism is sequestration: a greatly enlarged spleen retains a disproportionate fraction of each blood-cell population within its expanded sinusoidal bed, and simultaneously accelerates phagocytic destruction of opsonised or mildly damaged cells. The normal spleen holds roughly 30% of the platelet pool; in marked splenomegaly, up to 90% of platelets may pool in the spleen, producing thrombocytopenia even when platelet production is adequate. Hypersplenism most often accompanies portal hypertension from cirrhosis or schistosomiasis, but any cause of splenomegaly — haematological malignancy, storage diseases, infection, inflammatory conditions, or splenic vein thrombosis — can trigger it. The severity of hypersplenism broadly tracks the degree of splenomegaly, though the correlation is imperfect. Distinguishing hypersplenism from bone marrow suppression requires a marrow biopsy showing adequate cellularity, and from immune destruction requires specific antibody tests. Haematology, hepatology, and in some cases oncology are the specialties most involved.

typesClassification.description: The most common subtype, driven by congestive splenomegaly from cirrhosis, portal vein thrombosis, or schistosomiasis; thrombocytopenia predominates and is often the first indication of portal hypertension.

Lymphoma, leukaemia, and myeloproliferative neoplasms infiltrate the spleen directly, enlarging it and simultaneously causing marrow suppression; disentangling hypersplenism from malignant cytopenia can be difficult.
Malaria, visceral leishmaniasis, infectious mononucleosis, and other chronic infections cause reactive splenic enlargement with secondary sequestration cytopenias.
Gaucher disease and Niemann-Pick disease deposit substrate in splenic macrophages, massively enlarging the spleen and producing severe hypersplenism alongside marrow infiltration.

primarySymptoms: Fatigue and reduced exercise tolerance from anaemia caused by accelerated red cell sequestration and destruction in the enlarged spleen.
Easy bruising, petechiae, or prolonged bleeding after minor cuts, reflecting thrombocytopenia from excessive platelet pooling in the spleen.
Recurrent infections or slow recovery from illness, resulting from leukopenia — reduced white blood cells — when all cell lines are affected.
Left upper-quadrant fullness, pressure, or aching pain from the enlarged spleen occupying space under the left rib cage.
Early satiety and reduced appetite because a massively enlarged spleen compresses the stomach.
Visible or palpable abdominal mass in the left upper quadrant when splenomegaly is pronounced, detectable on clinical examination.
Pallor of the skin and conjunctivae accompanying symptomatic anaemia.
Peripheral blood count abnormalities: low platelets typically below 100 × 10⁹/L, low white cell count, and low haemoglobin, one or more depending on severity.
Symptoms of the underlying condition: jaundice and ascites in cirrhosis, lymphadenopathy in lymphoma, bone pain in storage diseases.
earlyWarningSigns: A platelet count consistently below 150 × 10⁹/L found on routine blood testing in a patient with liver disease.
Spleen palpable below the left costal margin on physical examination.
Unexplained anaemia or leukopenia in the absence of nutritional deficiency or medication effect.
Increasing left-sided abdominal discomfort after meals.
emergencySigns: Acute spontaneous splenic rupture — sudden severe left upper-quadrant pain radiating to the left shoulder, hypotension, and syncope; a surgical emergency.
Severe thrombocytopenic bleeding: spontaneous bruising across large areas, mucous membrane bleeding, or haemorrhage from an oesophageal varix in portal hypertension.
Signs of sepsis superimposed on leukopenia: rapid deterioration with fever and haemodynamic instability.

causes.description: Elevated portal venous pressure backs up into the splenic vein, causing progressive congestive enlargement of the spleen. The resulting splenomegaly traps platelets and red cells, producing the cytopenias of hypersplenism. Cirrhosis from any cause — alcohol, viral hepatitis, non-alcoholic fatty liver disease — accounts for the majority of cases in high-income countries.

Chronic infection with Schistosoma mansoni or S. japonicum causes periportal fibrosis and portal hypertension, leading to massive congestive splenomegaly and severe thrombocytopenia. It is the leading cause of hypersplenism in sub-Saharan Africa, the Middle East, and East Asia, where the infection is endemic.

Lymphomas, myeloproliferative neoplasms, leukaemias, and hairy cell leukaemia directly invade splenic parenchyma, enlarging the organ and accelerating cell destruction. Myelofibrosis, in particular, causes massive splenomegaly with marked hypersplenism through extramedullary haematopoiesis and fibrotic marrow displacement.

Conditions such as hereditary spherocytosis, thalassaemia, and sickle-cell disease require the spleen to process large volumes of abnormal or damaged red cells, which expands the organ and raises the rate of red cell and platelet destruction.

Enzyme deficiencies allow substrate to accumulate in splenic macrophages, producing the massive splenomegaly characteristic of Type 1 Gaucher disease; splenic volumes 5-50 times normal are reported and hypersplenism is a direct result.

Visceral leishmaniasis (kala-azar), chronic malaria, and infectious mononucleosis all produce reactive splenic enlargement through immune stimulation; hypersplenism may be severe in leishmaniasis, where it is a principal cause of anaemia and thrombocytopenia.

riskFactors.description: The degree of portal hypertension, and therefore hypersplenism, correlates with hepatic fibrosis stage; addressing alcohol use, treating viral hepatitis, and managing metabolic liver disease reduce progression risk.
Lymphoma and myeloproliferative neoplasms directly infiltrate and enlarge the spleen; risk is inherent to the malignancy.
Hereditary spherocytosis, thalassaemia syndromes, and sickle-cell disease cause chronic red cell destruction that progressively expands splenic workload and size.
Living in or travelling to endemic regions without adequate prophylaxis carries risk of the chronic infections that cause massive congestive splenomegaly.
Biallelic mutations in the GBA gene cause glucocerebrosidase deficiency; Type 1 Gaucher disease produces the most severe hypersplenism of any storage disease.
Thrombosis of the portal or splenic vein from any cause obstructs outflow, producing isolated left-sided portal hypertension and severe splenic congestion.

affectedDemographics: Adults with cirrhosis or chronic liver disease, the largest group globally
Children and adults in sub-Saharan Africa and Asia with hepatosplenic schistosomiasis
Patients with haematological malignancies, especially myeloproliferative neoplasms
Children with hereditary haemolytic anaemias such as hereditary spherocytosis or thalassaemia
People with Gaucher disease type 1, who present from childhood to middle age
People living in areas endemic for visceral leishmaniasis or hyperendemic malaria
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis rests on demonstrating the triad of splenomegaly, cytopenia of one or more cell lines, and adequate bone marrow cellularity. Splenomegaly is confirmed by physical examination and measured precisely by abdominal ultrasound or CT, which also identify the underlying hepatic or vascular cause. The full blood count quantifies the cytopenias; a platelet count below 100 × 10⁹/L in the presence of splenomegaly is the most common presentation. Bone marrow aspirate and trephine biopsy are performed when marrow suppression cannot be excluded on clinical grounds — the finding of normal or hyperplastic marrow in the face of peripheral cytopenia supports hypersplenism. Reticulocyte count is elevated in haemolytic hypersplenism, and a peripheral blood film may show red cell fragmentation, spherocytes, or target cells depending on the aetiology. Liver function tests, hepatitis serology, fibroscan or liver biopsy, and portal vein Doppler ultrasonography delineate the hepatic cause when present. In suspected storage disease, plasma glucocerebrosidase activity or lysosomal enzyme panels are diagnostic. MRI volumetric measurement of splenic volume is increasingly used to monitor disease and response to enzyme replacement therapy in Gaucher disease. Splenectomy, when performed, should be preceded by full workup to exclude marrow infiltration that would make the cytopenias non-reversible.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count with differential and reticulocytes
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies the degree of anaemia, thrombocytopenia, and leukopenia; elevated reticulocytes confirm peripheral red cell destruction rather than marrow failure.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard venous blood draw; results typically available within 2-4 hours at most hospitals.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms and quantifies splenomegaly, assesses portal venous flow, and identifies hepatic cirrhosis, portal or splenic vein thrombosis, or infiltrative lesions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A non-invasive, painless ultrasound scan of the abdomen performed with the patient lying flat; takes 20-30 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Bone marrow aspirate and trephine biopsy
diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes hypersplenism (hypercellular or normal marrow) from marrow suppression or infiltration by malignancy; essential before attributing cytopenia purely to splenic sequestration.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A minor procedure under local anaesthetic; a needle is inserted into the posterior iliac crest and samples aspirated and cored. Mild discomfort and bruising are expected; results take 5-7 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Liver function tests and hepatitis serology
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the most common underlying cause — chronic liver disease — and guides its management alongside hypersplenism treatment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; a hepatitis B surface antigen, anti-HCV, and standard liver biochemistry panel returned within 24 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides precise splenic volume measurement, identifies varices, detects malignant infiltration, and guides planning for embolisation or splenectomy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 5-10 minute scan after intravenous contrast injection; mildly uncomfortable; iodine allergy must be flagged.

diagnosticTests.test: Plasma glucocerebrosidase or lysosomal enzyme panel
diagnosticTests.purpose: Diagnoses Gaucher disease or other lysosomal storage diseases when there is massive organomegaly without obvious haematological or hepatic cause.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A specialised blood test sent to a metabolic diseases laboratory; results take 1-2 weeks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects abnormal red cell morphology — spherocytes, target cells, fragmented cells — that help identify the mechanism of haemolysis underlying hypersplenism.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Same blood draw as the full blood count; the film is reviewed under microscope by a haematologist.

treatmentOverview: The guiding principle is that hypersplenism is a secondary problem; treating the underlying cause is the primary goal. For cirrhotic portal hypertension, optimising liver disease management and non-selective beta-blockers that reduce portal pressure can limit further splenic enlargement. Non-variceal bleeding in this context is managed through portal pressure reduction. Enzyme replacement therapy (imiglucerase, velaglucerase alfa) for Gaucher disease reduces splenic volume by 30-50% over 1-2 years and reverses hypersplenism significantly without requiring splenectomy. Treating schistosomiasis with praziquantel halts the stimulus for progressive fibrosis. When cytopenias are clinically significant — thrombocytopenia causing bleeding, anaemia requiring transfusion, or leukopenia causing serious infections — and the underlying cause cannot be treated effectively, direct splenic interventions are considered. Partial splenic embolisation (PSE) occludes 40-70% of splenic arterial supply, reducing splenic volume and raising platelet counts in 70-90% of patients, while preserving residual immune function and avoiding the full risks of splenectomy. Surgical splenectomy remains the most durable treatment, correcting cytopenias in over 90% of cases, but carries perioperative risk, particularly in patients with portal hypertension and impaired coagulation. Thrombopoietin receptor agonists (eltrombopag, avatrombopag) are now used to raise platelet counts pre-procedurally in thrombocytopenic cirrhotic patients, reducing the need for splenectomy. Transfusion support for severe anaemia or thrombocytopenic bleeding provides temporary stabilisation. All patients who undergo splenectomy must receive the same vaccination and antibiotic prophylaxis protocol as for any other cause of asplenia.

medicalTreatments.name: Treatment of underlying cause
medicalTreatments.description: Viral hepatitis treatment (direct-acting antivirals for HCV, antiviral therapy for HBV), alcohol abstinence, or praziquantel for schistosomiasis addresses the root driver of portal hypertension and may partially reverse splenomegaly over months to years.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: HCV cure with direct-acting antivirals improves thrombocytopenia in approximately 40-60% of cirrhotic patients over 1-2 years; outcomes depend on residual liver function.

medicalTreatments.name: Non-selective beta-blockers (propranolol, carvedilol)
medicalTreatments.description: Reduce portal venous pressure in cirrhotic portal hypertension, limiting progressive splenic enlargement; standard of care in patients with oesophageal varices and may slow the progression of hypersplenism.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces portal pressure by approximately 15-25%; reduces variceal bleeding risk by roughly 40-50%, with some parallel benefit to splenic congestion.

medicalTreatments.name: Enzyme replacement therapy (ERT) for Gaucher disease
medicalTreatments.description: Imiglucerase or velaglucerase alfa delivered intravenously every two weeks; reduces splenic volume and substantially corrects cytopenias, typically avoiding splenectomy in newly diagnosed patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces splenic volume by 30-50% at 1 year and up to 80% at 5 years; platelet count normalises in approximately 70-80% of patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Thrombopoietin receptor agonists (eltrombopag, avatrombopag)
medicalTreatments.description: Oral agents that stimulate platelet production; licensed for raising platelet counts in thrombocytopenic chronic liver disease patients before scheduled procedures, reducing peri-procedural bleeding risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Avatrombopag raises platelets above 50 × 10⁹/L in approximately 65-70% of patients within 5 days of the procedure — sufficient to avoid transfusion in most cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Partial splenic embolisation (PSE)
medicalTreatments.description: Interventional radiological procedure injecting embolic material into splenic arterial branches to infarct 40-70% of parenchyma; preferred over splenectomy in cirrhotic patients because it preserves immune function and avoids surgery.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Raises platelet count by 50-100% in approximately 70-90% of patients; effect durable for 1-3 years in most series; repeat embolisation is feasible.

surgicalOptions.name: Splenectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical removal of the spleen, performed laparoscopically when feasible; the most durable treatment for hypersplenism-associated cytopenias when conservative measures fail or when massive splenomegaly causes mechanical symptoms.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Corrects cytopenias in over 90% of cases; operative mortality under 1% in non-cirrhotic patients but rises to 5-10% in decompensated cirrhosis.

surgicalOptions.name: Transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt (TIPS)
surgicalOptions.description: Radiological creation of a channel between the portal and hepatic veins to decompress the portal system; reduces splenic congestion and improves cytopenias as a secondary benefit in cirrhotic portal hypertension.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Reduces portal pressure by approximately 50% and improves thrombocytopenia in 60-80% of cirrhotic patients; primary indication is variceal bleeding or refractory ascites.

alternativeTreatments.name: Splenic artery embolisation monitoring
alternativeTreatments.description: Partial embolisation serves as a middle ground between medical management and splenectomy; ongoing surveillance of splenic volume and blood counts guides the need for repeat intervention.

alternativeTreatments.name: Nutritional support
alternativeTreatments.description: In cirrhosis-associated hypersplenism, optimising nutrition including branched-chain amino acids may reduce sarcopenia risk and improve overall hepatic reserve, indirectly stabilising the severity of hypersplenism.

whySeeSpecialist: A haematologist coordinates the evaluation of cytopenias and performs bone marrow assessment when needed. A hepatologist manages the portal hypertension that drives most cases. Interventional radiology is involved for embolisation decisions. When malignancy is the cause, an oncologist leads treatment. Early specialist referral avoids unnecessary splenectomy in patients whose cytopenias may respond to medical management.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a haematologist working within a multidisciplinary team that includes hepatology and interventional radiology. For Gaucher disease, seek a metabolic specialist with access to enzyme replacement therapy. For cirrhotic hypersplenism, a hepatologist with experience in portal hypertension management and familiarity with thrombopoietin receptor agonists is particularly valuable.
preventionStrategies: Treat and monitor underlying liver disease before portal hypertension and splenomegaly become established.
Complete schistosomiasis treatment with praziquantel and avoid freshwater exposure in endemic regions.
Begin enzyme replacement therapy for Gaucher disease early to prevent massive splenomegaly.
Limit alcohol consumption to prevent progression of alcoholic liver disease.
Vaccinate against hepatitis B in non-immune individuals to prevent viral cirrhosis.
Use antimalarial prophylaxis in endemic areas to prevent chronic malaria-related splenomegaly.
lifestyleModifications: Avoid contact sports or abdominal trauma, which can rupture a massively enlarged spleen.
Report any sudden severe left-sided abdominal pain immediately — it may indicate splenic rupture.
Abstain from alcohol completely if liver disease is the underlying cause.
Maintain adequate nutrition with support from a dietitian, particularly in cirrhotic patients.
Take prescribed portal pressure-reducing medications (beta-blockers) consistently.
Attend regular blood count monitoring to track the evolution of cytopenias.

recommended: High-protein, nutrient-dense diet to support bone marrow compensatory haematopoiesis
Adequate folate and vitamin B12 to support red cell production
Iron-rich foods if iron-deficiency anaemia co-exists
Low-sodium diet in cirrhotic patients to manage ascites
avoid: Alcohol in any quantity when liver disease is the underlying cause
Raw shellfish and undercooked seafood, which pose infection risk in immunocompromised or leukopenic patients
Excess sodium that worsens fluid retention in portal hypertension

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate exercise is encouraged to maintain muscle mass and general health. Contact sports, heavy lifting, and activities with risk of abdominal impact should be avoided when massive splenomegaly is present because of the risk of splenic rupture. Swimming and low-impact aerobic exercise are generally safe. In cirrhotic patients, exercise tolerance should be gauged to current hepatic reserve.
dailyManagement: Monitor for bleeding symptoms — unusual bruising, petechiae, prolonged bleeding from cuts — and report promptly.
Attend scheduled blood counts to track cytopenia severity and response to treatment.
Take portal pressure medications at consistent times if prescribed.
Inform your surgeon or dentist of thrombocytopenia before any procedure.
Know the signs of splenic rupture: sudden severe left shoulder or flank pain with faintness, requiring emergency services.
prognosis: The prognosis of hypersplenism is primarily determined by the underlying condition rather than by the splenic overactivity itself. In cirrhosis, prognosis reflects hepatic reserve as scored by Child-Pugh or MELD; compensated cirrhosis carries a 10-year survival above 50%, while decompensated disease has a median survival of 1-2 years without transplant. Isolated mild thrombocytopenia from portal hypertension rarely causes life-threatening bleeding without a precipitant such as variceal rupture. In Gaucher disease, enzyme replacement therapy transforms prognosis dramatically, allowing normal or near-normal life expectancy. In haematological malignancy, treatment of the primary disease is the main prognostic determinant. After successful splenectomy or effective partial embolisation, cytopenias resolve in over 90% of patients. However, splenectomy creates a lifelong need for infection prophylaxis that must be maintained indefinitely.
recoveryTimeline: After partial splenic embolisation, platelet counts begin rising within 1-2 weeks and peak at 4-8 weeks; patients typically go home within 2-5 days, with post-embolisation syndrome (fever and abdominal pain) lasting up to 2 weeks. Splenectomy provides more immediate and durable correction; platelet counts normalise within days to weeks, with full post-operative recovery in 3-6 weeks for laparoscopic surgery. In Gaucher disease, enzyme replacement therapy improves spleen volume and cytopenias over 1-3 years of continuous treatment.
complications: Spontaneous splenic rupture in massive splenomegaly — a surgical emergency with high mortality.
Severe thrombocytopenic bleeding, including gastrointestinal haemorrhage from oesophageal varices in cirrhotic patients.
Increased infection susceptibility from leukopenia, or — after splenectomy — from acquired asplenia requiring lifelong prophylaxis.
Post-embolisation syndrome after splenic artery embolisation: fever, left upper-quadrant pain, ileus, lasting 1-2 weeks.
Thromboembolic events after splenectomy, particularly portal vein thrombosis, occurring in 5-15% of splenectomy patients with underlying haematological disease.
Persisting cytopenias if the underlying marrow disease was not fully accounted for before splenectomy.

supportResources.name: British Liver Trust — Portal Hypertension and Hypersplenism
supportResources.description: Patient-facing guidance on portal hypertension, splenomegaly, and related haematological complications.

supportResources.name: National Gaucher Foundation
supportResources.description: Patient support and education for Gaucher disease, the storage disease most commonly associated with severe hypersplenism.

supportResources.name: NHS — Spleen problems and spleen removal
supportResources.description: UK patient guide to splenic conditions including the consequences of enlarged and removed spleens.

sources.title: Aster RH, Bougie DW. Drug-induced immune thrombocytopenia. NEJM 2007 (context: platelet physiology and splenic sequestration)
Giannini EG. Review article: Thrombocytopenia in chronic liver disease and pharmacologic treatment options. Alimentary Pharmacology & Therapeutics 2006
Mela M et al. EASL Clinical Practice Guidelines: Liver cirrhosis. Journal of Hepatology 2021
Zimran A et al. Treatment of Gaucher disease: Where do we go from here? Blood Reviews 2020
Simonetti RG et al. Partial splenic embolisation in portal hypertension: a systematic review. Hepatology International 2015
AASLD Practice Guidance — Portal Hypertension and Varices. Hepatology 2022

confusedWithConditions.name: Hyposplenism
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hyposplenism is reduced splenic function causing vulnerability to infection; hypersplenism is overactivity causing cytopenias — the two are physiologically opposite states.

confusedWithConditions.name: Aplastic anaemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Aplastic anaemia causes pancytopenia through marrow failure, so the marrow biopsy is hypocellular; in hypersplenism the marrow is normal or hyperplastic and splenomegaly is present.

confusedWithConditions.name: Anaemia from other causes
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Anaemia in hypersplenism is always accompanied by splenomegaly and usually by thrombocytopenia; isolated anaemia without splenomegaly points to nutritional, haemolytic, or marrow causes rather than splenic sequestration.

relatedConditions.name: Hyposplenism
relatedConditions.relevance: The clinical opposite of hypersplenism; both represent extremes of splenic functional dysregulation.

relatedConditions.name: Myelofibrosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Causes massive splenomegaly through extramedullary haematopoiesis and is one of the most severe causes of hypersplenism.

relatedConditions.name: Osteomyelofibrosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Bone marrow fibrosis displaces haematopoiesis to the spleen, driving splenomegaly and secondary hypersplenism.

relatedConditions.name: Waldenström macroglobulinaemia
relatedConditions.relevance: This B-cell lymphoproliferative disease frequently infiltrates the spleen, causing hypersplenism alongside its haematological features.

faqs.question: What is hypersplenism?
faqs.answer: Hypersplenism is when an enlarged spleen destroys blood cells too quickly, causing low counts of red cells, platelets, or white cells. The bone marrow tries to compensate by working harder. It is always caused by an underlying disease that has enlarged the spleen.

faqs.question: What causes hypersplenism?
faqs.answer: The most common cause worldwide is portal hypertension from liver disease, especially cirrhosis, which congests and enlarges the spleen. Other causes include haematological malignancies infiltrating the spleen, Gaucher disease, hereditary haemolytic anaemias, schistosomiasis, and visceral leishmaniasis.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of hypersplenism?
faqs.answer: Symptoms include fatigue from anaemia, easy bruising and petechiae from thrombocytopenia, recurrent infections from low white cells, left upper-abdominal pain or fullness from the enlarged spleen, and early satiety. Symptoms of the underlying condition — such as jaundice in liver disease — are also present.

faqs.question: How is hypersplenism diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires a low blood count (anaemia, thrombocytopenia, or leukopenia), splenomegaly confirmed by ultrasound or CT, and a bone marrow biopsy showing normal or increased cellularity. Finding the underlying cause is equally important and requires liver function tests, hepatitis serology, and sometimes marrow analysis.

faqs.question: Does hypersplenism always need surgery?
faqs.answer: No. Most patients are managed by treating the underlying condition. Surgery or partial splenic embolisation is reserved for severe cytopenias causing bleeding or infections that cannot be controlled medically. Enzyme replacement therapy avoids splenectomy in the majority of Gaucher disease patients.

faqs.question: What is partial splenic embolisation?
faqs.answer: Partial splenic embolisation is an interventional radiology procedure where embolic material is injected into splenic arteries to destroy 40-70% of the spleen. It raises platelet and blood cell counts in most patients while preserving some immune function, avoiding the risks of full splenectomy.

faqs.question: What is the risk of splenic rupture?
faqs.answer: Spontaneous rupture is rare but possible when the spleen is massively enlarged. It causes sudden severe left-sided abdominal pain radiating to the left shoulder, dizziness, and rapid deterioration. Contact sports and abdominal trauma should be avoided. Seek emergency care immediately for sudden left-sided pain.

faqs.question: Can liver disease cause hypersplenism?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cirrhosis is the most common cause of hypersplenism in high-income countries. Portal hypertension from cirrhosis backs pressure up into the splenic vein, enlarging the spleen and causing it to trap and destroy platelets, leading to thrombocytopenia in up to 70% of cirrhotic patients.

faqs.question: What happens to platelets in hypersplenism?
faqs.answer: A healthy spleen holds about 30% of the platelet pool. In hypersplenism, a greatly enlarged spleen can trap up to 90% of circulating platelets, dramatically reducing the platelet count in the blood despite normal platelet production by the bone marrow.

faqs.question: Is hypersplenism curable?
faqs.answer: It depends on the cause. Hypersplenism from treatable conditions — hepatitis C cured by direct-acting antivirals, Gaucher disease treated with enzyme replacement, or schistosomiasis cured with praziquantel — can fully resolve. Cirrhosis-related hypersplenism may improve if liver disease stabilises but is not always fully reversible.

faqs.question: What is the difference between splenomegaly and hypersplenism?
faqs.answer: Splenomegaly means an enlarged spleen on imaging; it does not require any functional abnormality. Hypersplenism means the enlarged spleen is actively destroying blood cells at an excessive rate, producing cytopenias. Splenomegaly is a sign; hypersplenism is a syndrome that requires splenomegaly plus cytopenia plus a reactive bone marrow.

faqs.question: If I have hypersplenism do I need the same vaccinations as someone without a spleen?
faqs.answer: If a splenectomy is planned or performed for hypersplenism, then yes — full asplenic vaccination including pneumococcal, meningococcal, Haemophilus influenzae type b, and annual flu vaccines is required, along with antibiotic prophylaxis guidance.

faqs.question: Can hypersplenism cause dangerous bleeding?
faqs.answer: Severe thrombocytopenia from hypersplenism can increase bleeding risk. Spontaneous bleeding from very low platelet counts below 20 × 10⁹/L can occur, but most patients with portal hypertension-related hypersplenism have platelet counts in the range 50-100 × 10⁹/L where spontaneous bleeding is uncommon without an additional trigger such as a variceal bleed.

faqs.question: Can hypersplenism affect children?
faqs.answer: Yes. Children with hereditary spherocytosis, thalassaemia, Gaucher disease, or haematological malignancies can develop hypersplenism. Paediatric haematologists manage these cases, and splenectomy is often delayed as long as possible in young children because of the long-term infection risk.

faqs.question: What is the bone marrow like in hypersplenism?
faqs.answer: In hypersplenism the bone marrow is typically normal or hyperplastic — it is working harder to compensate for peripheral blood cell destruction. This distinguishes hypersplenism from aplastic anaemia, where the marrow is hypocellular, or from malignant infiltration, where abnormal cells replace normal marrow.

faqs.question: How long does it take platelets to recover after splenectomy for hypersplenism?
faqs.answer: Platelet counts usually begin rising within days of splenectomy and often surge to above normal levels — sometimes above 1,000 × 10⁹/L — within the first 2-4 weeks. This thrombocytosis usually resolves over weeks to months. Anticoagulation is sometimes recommended during the peak to prevent portal vein thrombosis.

faqs.question: Can hypersplenism occur without liver disease?
faqs.answer: Yes. Haematological malignancies such as lymphoma, myelofibrosis, and hairy cell leukaemia are important non-hepatic causes. Gaucher disease, hereditary haemolytic anaemias, chronic infections like leishmaniasis, and splenic vein thrombosis also cause hypersplenism in the absence of liver disease.

faqs.question: What is the difference between hypersplenism and autoimmune thrombocytopenia?
faqs.answer: Both cause low platelets, but the mechanisms differ. Autoimmune thrombocytopenia (ITP) is caused by antibodies destroying platelets, usually without significant splenomegaly. Hypersplenism causes platelet sequestration and destruction in a massively enlarged spleen, with identifiable underlying disease and usually other cytopenias alongside the thrombocytopenia.

faqs.question: Do I need to see a haematologist for hypersplenism?
faqs.answer: Yes. A haematologist assesses the cytopenias and performs or interprets bone marrow biopsy when needed. They work alongside a hepatologist or other specialist managing the underlying disease. Decisions about splenectomy or embolisation require input from haematology, hepatology, and interventional radiology together.

faqs.question: What monitoring do I need if I have hypersplenism?
faqs.answer: Regular full blood counts to track cytopenia severity, scheduled abdominal ultrasounds to monitor splenic size, and periodic assessment of the underlying condition. If platelets fall below 50 × 10⁹/L, procedures and medicines that increase bleeding risk need to be managed carefully. Your specialist will set the review interval based on severity.

metaDescription: Hypersplenism is an overactive enlarged spleen that destroys blood cells, causing anaemia and low platelets. Learn causes, diagnosis, and treatment options.
